flashcards.front: What are the three keywords used for exception handling in C++?
flashcards.back: try, catch, and throw. The try block contains code that may throw an exception, catch blocks handle specific exception types, and throw keyword is used to raise an exception.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax of a try-catch block in C++?
flashcards.back: try { // code that may throw exception } catch (exception_type e) { // handler code } - Multiple catch blocks can handle different exception types.

flashcards.front: What is stack unwinding in C++ exception handling?
flashcards.back: When an exception is thrown, C++ destroys all local objects in the call stack from the throw point to the handler, calling destructors for each object. This ensures proper cleanup of resources.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between catch(...) and catch(Type e)?
flashcards.back: catch(Type e) catches only exceptions of specified type, while catch(...) catches all exceptions of any type. The ellipsis form is a catch-all handler.

flashcards.front: What is exception rethrowing and how is it done?
flashcards.back: Rethrowing an exception means throwing the current exception again using 'throw;' (without an object). This passes the exception to an outer try-catch block for further handling.

flashcards.front: What are the standard exception classes in C++?
flashcards.back: std::exception is the base class. Derived classes include: std::bad_alloc, std::runtime_error, std::logic_error, std::out_of_range, std::invalid_argument, std::length_error.

flashcards.front: What is the noexcept specifier in C++?
flashcards.back: noexcept specifies that a function cannot throw exceptions. noexcept(expression) makes it conditional. Functions marked noexcept have optimization benefits for the compiler.

flashcards.front: What is exception safety in C++?
flashcards.back: Exception safety guarantees: 1) No-throw guarantee (never throws), 2) Strong guarantee (operation succeeds or has no effect), 3) Basic guarantee (object remains in valid state), 4) No guarantee.

flashcards.front: How do you create a custom exception class?
flashcards.back: Derive a class from std::exception (or one of its derived classes) and override the what() method to return a descriptive message string.

flashcards.front: What happens if no catch block matches the thrown exception?
flashcards.back: The program calls std::terminate() which by default calls std::abort(), terminating the program abnormally. This is why proper exception handling is critical.

flashcards.front: What is the order of catch block execution?
flashcards.back: Catch blocks are evaluated sequentially from top to bottom. The first matching catch block executes. More specific exception types should come before general ones.

flashcards.front: Can exceptions be thrown from destructors?
flashcards.back: Yes, but it is strongly discouraged. If a destructor throws during stack unwinding, std::terminate() is called. Destructors should typically be noexcept.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between throw; and throw exception_object;?
flashcards.back: throw; rethrows the current exception (preserves original exception), while throw exception_object; creates and throws a new exception object.

flashcards.front: What is function try block in C++?
flashcards.back: A function try block associates a catch block with an entire function body. Syntax: ret_type func() try { body } catch(...) { handler } - Useful for constructors.
